sentences.text: Just from the boat, yeah.
No, it's-- people come down for a few days, they stand out here.
Most of them go home.
Very seldom do they catch anything.
That's the main reason.
And just waste a whole day, just stand there.
You won't to see some action.
You go out for four hours.
There are some people with boats here too, but you know, the old story.
And most of them are not captains, you know?
My uncle used to say, by me you're a captain.
By your mother, you're a captain.
But between captains, you're no captain.
You know?
That it was his story.
[LAUGHTER] And that's the same here.
It was fate to go out.
Same here.
At my age, go to-- belong to the JCC.
We exercise, meet friends, go out for lunch.
Unfortunately, I think as I told you, I'm-- whatever you want to call it punishment or whatever, I don't know for what-- I have a son who needs more supervision.
So I feel like somebody asked me, why don't you volunteer more for the hospital over there right now?
Which I would, but I have my own son to take care of.
Oh, yeah he was born problem with the-- not educable.
He holds a job, but under supervision.
He lives by himself, independent.
No, no.
Not autistic.
Just not educable.
Very set in this ways.
Independent, very independent.
You know, there's certain terms for him.
Whatever it is, he's not-- can't fit into to society.
OK.
My name is Harry, H-A-double R-Y, Konar, K-O-N-A-R.
I was born in Maciejowice, Poland.
Maciejowice is close to Warsaw, near the Vistula.
I was born in June of 1921.
I was one of four children.
I had an older sister and two younger brothers.
We only stayed in the town till about I was five or six years old.
And since my father's family had moved by this time to Radom.
They were in the leather business.
From the five, six years living there, I don't remember too much.
All I know that I was told that at three, I went to cheder there.
And time was taken up just visiting family.
We lived in a house that was owned by my grandfather, which is still standing.
And I was there a few years ago to see it.
Maciejowice, to the best of my own collection, it M-A-C-I-E-J-O-W-I-C-E.
There wasn't much.
We used to go fishing there.
I can remember going to a creek to swim in those few years when we were kids.
But our life was-- we didn't know anything different or anything better, so it was OK.
We always had enough food.
I can remember my, my grandfather's house.
There were so many rooms there to run-- to run around and play inside or out.
Milk was really easy.
He had a cow outside.
And we had our own milk.
He used to have a very large garden that he planted and raised their own vegetables.
But at this time, he and my mother's older brother, they were in business together, and another uncle.
I wouldn't call them farmers because they rented the land, a lot of land.
They used to raise fish.
And they had a lot of those, carp mainly, which is a Polish staple, a European staple.
And all I can remember, we used to go feed the fish.
That was a treat for us kids to put on the wagon and spend the whole day.
And they were laid out like small waterways connected-- interconnected.
They were no more than 10 feet wide to 50 to 80 feet long.
And the way I understood it, they were raised up like a little dam and let the water out.
And then they just picked the fish out when the time came at the end of the season.
When winter set on, they used to gather all the fish and then ship it off to Warsaw.
And farmers used to line up.
It looked like 100, maybe 1,000 of little horse and buggy wagons with bells.
And they send it off.
And it was a very, very good life for us.
My uncle and my grandfather, they rented the land from somebody, from I, I don't even know.
They didn't own it, no.
At this point, I didn't know.
In later years, I think that things have changed.
But in those days, I was too young to realize what it meant.
I found out later that they owned-- that they rented the land.
And they rent land for other purposes too.
They used to grow hay or feed c we used to call-- the only Polish word I know for it was [POLISH].
And by the end of the summer season, they would sell to the farmers an acre or two, and the farmer would go out and cut it themselves and take it away.
And they used to raise hop, hop for beer, what you make beer of.
But we were not-- kids, were not involved in this.
We only knew what was going on.
From landowners.
Once it was grown.
They would do the growing themselves.
My, my grandfather, my uncles, the family.
And then they would sell an acre at a time, but they had to come and cut it themselves on certain items.
The fish, they would pick up themselves.
I know there was a laboratory-- we were not allowed to go in there as kids-- where they raised the minnows or so on because of-- I assume-- contamination and so.
But the hay, they sold.
The hop, they sold.
And then they also gave rise-- I can remember like in a dream-- they allowed the Pole-- the Polish army to come once a year or twice a year to shoot pheasants and so on.
And they would line up.
It was a city square.
It was a small town.
Population of Jewish people was no more than 200 people.
And it was mostly Jewish.
The farmers, the Polish people were on the outlying area of this little village.
The square with four streets, that's what I can remember.

I mean like in a dream, to remember to see the soldiers that come with their big trucks full of dead pheasants after they stopped over at the house to say goodbye or whatever to the officers over there.
When I was about five or six, my father decided to move in to Radom to the bigger city.
And like any big city, it was different school.
At this point, I still went to cheder over there for-- now, I don't recall if it was for a year or two-- for a short while.
A big city was different.
We didn't have any street cars.
It was all horse and wagon still.
But you saw a few cars around.
We had electricity, which we didn't have in the small village.
We had radios, but no electricity.
They would operate on batteries.
Yeah.
In the bigger city, you had more of it.
You could rent what we called in Polish, a [POLISH], which a horse and one of those that you see in England with the-- that they drive to occasions.
The carriages, yeah.
And you took those to go to the train station, to go to the bus station.
They were-- they were on the corners, just like cabs today.
And life wasn't bad until about 1932.
My father died.
[SOBBING] I was 11 years old.
This was a tragedy.
My mother went on.
She took over the business, which was a wholesale business.
She went on until the war ended-- till the war started.
We lived-- we didn't have houses.
It was all apartments.
We lived in a building that was, I think, four stories high.
The lower level were-- the front were stores and the-- behind the stores were more for-- called it warehouse, now wholesale.
And we had a place like this, a big, bigger than this apartment for the wholesale.
And we also had a man working for us.
And he actually had an area cordoned off, and he lived there.
For what reason?
Because we didn't have any room upstairs or whatever, but keep him separate.
So he has his freedom.
He would come and go.
And the first few years, I remember we had a man, he was from the hometown, from Maciejowice, that my father did a favor to a friend that he took him in.
And he helped out in business.
In Radom, life was a lot different.
After a year or two, I changed over to a different school.
No more-- also cheder, but half the day was-- we had-- spoke Polish and the teachers.
And the other half, we used to go home for lunch, because our big meal was always at noon, 1:00 or 2:00.
And then we'd go back and have Hebrew lessons and classes and so on.
Approximately in 1938, my mother decided that I should also get a better education and in a Talmud.
Yes?
Well, when my father started the business, at first-- I say when they started because he had the business before '28 approximately when he came to Radom.
He was on the [INAUDIBLE].
But in either in '29 or '30, from-- we start to be modern.
From the factories, he got what we call in German [GERMAN], a franchise from a certain factory.
And he sold only their goods.
He sold it to shoe makers, to shoe factories, anybody who could use.
And only the soft leather, not the hard leather.
He sold it to anybody who could use this type of material.
And then when my mother took over, the man by this point-- for reasons I don't remember-- left.
And she got somebody either more capable or so on, a local man from the city that we knew.
And he worked with her.
But we were kids.
We were all busy going to school, going to cheder.
The few hours we had at night to play with our friends and Shabbas.
Naturally, Shabbas was observed 100%, not like-- not like here, you know?
Nobody in the city of Radom, there were approximately close to 30,000 Jewish families.
The city, at this time, had a population total of between 110 and 120 with outlying area-- Christians, no.
30,000 Families.
Yeah.
I'm almost sure.
Yeah, we had a lot of Jewish people.
We had a lot of temples and a lot of shuls.
The inner city was.
No, it wasn't because my uncles and my cousins didn't live near us.
They lived more-- when we went to visit them, it was just like here.
If it was 10% or 20%, I don't recall.
It was spread out.
But the farmers and everything else was in Polish hands, landowners.
The Jewish people from way back were in business mostly.
When you went down on [NON-ENGLISH] Street, the store were strictly Jewish stores.

But very-- even in my time, very seldom did you see Jewish people or more-- less Jewish people with beards and the Jewish caps and so on, than from what I read now and what I understand happened many years before this.
They were more modern, even my own cousins.
My father's-- one of the oldest brother who had nine kids, and there were four sons.
Only one son was with a beard, the yeshiva.
The rest of them were modern like today, you know?
That's--
The story I can remember, one Sunday, my mother's brother came to visit us from Warsaw on the train.
And my uncle, who had a small little beard-- which I showed you in the picture-- and my uncle criticized him.
And they laughed and joked with him because Europeans were very courteous people.
When you came on a bus or train, if a family saw an older person come in-- doesn't have to be old, older than 30 or 40-- and they share the-- kids sit and she made them stand up and give the seat.
Well my uncle got a seat right away, my mother's brother.
But my other uncle didn't because he was a little beard.
You know there was a difference.
Jewish people were treated differently.
No two ways about it.
We walked all over.
We went all over.
But we were not harassed, not picked on or so on.
Sometimes somebody would say a dirty word, dirty Jew, you know, or so on, but you didn't pay attention to it.
Oh yeah, he looked like a Jew, definitely.
Definitely.
This was a way of life.
The coaches, the wagons-- and that started in approximately '35 or '36-- had a metal plate on their caps that saying, [NON-ENGLISH], Christian driver.
They want to make sure that no Christian or Pole drives with a Jew, you know?
It started off in the last few years.
This didn't start until '36 or '37 when the things started in Germany.
And you knew the anti-Semitism grew a lot.
But I didn't have no problem.
We traveled all over.
We went all over.
We went to visit my grandfather every summer.
We took the train.
We didn't have no problem.
To Maciewjowice, you know?
And sometimes we went to Warsaw because there was a lot of family there, on a train.
There was no, no problem going anyplace.
We didn't have oranges, only when they were in season because you didn't import a lot.
My father used to make wine from grapes himself.
I don't remember.
I'm sure it wasn't the stores and so on.
But for Yom Tov, he made his own wine.
If it was a custom to do it this way, this is what they did.
We ate mostly-- we ate fish.
We especially ate fish because whenever my grandfather came to visit or somebody, they brought the fish from town.
But we ate a lot of chicken, a lot of meat.
But we had eggs, milk.
There was no problem.
It was different style.
You didn't start in the morning eating until you went to the bakery and bought fresh bread.
And at night, the same thing.
Now, we used to have our big meal at noon.
At night, you just had something very light.
But we had very-- I don't remember starving.
We all looked very good.
We were not-- by, by, by here, maybe Europe was considered poor.
Cow meat, you know, beef.
No problem.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah, we had cheese.
Not been assortment as you see here in the store.
Used to eat a lot of cottage cheese.
We had all kinds of food, but not this assortment as we have now.
And then came-- we knew the war started-- was going to come because I can remember our neighbors, the elderly people, talking that's going to be bad.
And the war is going to last six, seven years.
And Germany is going to lose.
You know, schmoozing, politicking, a lot of.
But we didn't have no idea of what war was and what it's going to be.
In 1938, my mother decided I should go for a few years to a yeshiva.
And there's one yeshiva in Poland which is very well known in Lublin, Yeshiva Chachmei Chachmei Lublin.
What it means is the yeshiva of the educated in Lublin.
As a matter of fact, the Rabbi Shapiro started it in the early '30s, I think.
And I can remember him making a speech for us one day.
He came to America in '34 or '35 or something like this.
And somebody asked him a question, he says, you're going to graduate 500 students a year.
Where are you going to take cities for them?
There's not enough cities for so many rabbis.
And he says no, he's only going to graduate 500, but two are going to be rabbis, and 498 are going to be critics.
Critics.
That's what he told us.
It was his famous saying.
And this is what he said in the United States in the '30s.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Now I know that to get into this yeshiva, you had to go through a class for approximately a year, year and a half before you go in.
So they established, I think there was about 20 or 30 of us outside the Lublin yeshiva, not too far in a little village maybe 10 or 15 kilometer from.
And we were supposed to study there.
But we went into Lublin quite often, at least once a month for different occasions.
And then in '39, I didn't go back anymore because the war was starting.
And this is when it became bad.
Yeah.
They were good times.
[SOBBING] It's not just the yeshiva, whenever I talk about life.
Brings back bad memories.
How life was.
You know, I had a family.
[CRYING] You lost everything.
[SOBBING] I know a lot of people it's easy to talk about it, but it isn't.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Then I had a sister, my older sister.
She got married in '37 or '38.
And my brother-in-law had a brother who had left Radom many, many years before this-- 10, 15, or 20.
And then, all of a sudden, the Germans shipped out all the Polish Jews back to Poland and a lot of other ones like this.
And he filled us in what was-- he was from Leipzig-- and he filled us in what was going on there.
And it was worse than we imagined.
There were-- I mean it showed Kristallnacht and all this stuff.
But they were-- you were discriminated against in school and wherever you went on a large scale.
You couldn't do this.
You couldn't do this because you were a Jew.
You were verboten, forbidden to go to college.
And in Poland, while it was true they didn't allow Jews to go to medical school or, or, or lawyer school or whatever.
But a lot of them, they took a certain percentage.
And my two cousins in Radom, Sam, his sisters, younger sisters went to college, graduated, you know.
Yes, they were far or few in between, but some of them were accepted.
But in Germany it put a stop to everything after Hitler came to power.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The joke was going back to Poland he says, do we have enough to eat?
Did we have a good life?
No, it wasn't for everybody.
There were a lot of poor people.
But they were poor because the country was poor and discriminated, and you didn't have a chance to grow, to get an education.
I can remember the elder people talk and a lot of people who escaped from Poland either they go bankrupt or they embezzled something or so on.
But otherwise, if you wanted to work, you could make a living.
Well, how true it was, I don't know anymore because there was hatred.
The church hated us.
There was anti-Semitism.
There's no doubt about it.
But I know there's a man right here has two brothers from Florida-- in Florida from my hometown.
They're both college graduates.
Their father was a chemist in the leather factory.
The original name was Lip-- as a matter of fact, he puts a magazine out now.
The original name home was Lipschitz.
Now it's Lipson.
You'll see him in some magazines.
I knew them from home, you know, and a lot of them.
So this is short of what happened Poland.
We didn't have-- we lived in the same place.
We didn't have no problems.
My parents dealt with Christians.
It turned out later, I found out they were not Christians, they were what we called Volksdeutsche.
They were Germans who lived in the-- but they were still Polish.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Then the war started in the end of '39, and things took a turn.
When Germans came in, from the day they came in, they were looking for Jews.
First, for Jews with beards, and they shaved them off.
They made spectacles out of them.

Get them into a-- gather them in a place and made them kneel and so on while somebody-- they had-- while they shave their beards off and they had a picnic with it, and the beatings, and everything else.
No, no.
I do have some pictures that I took up for magazines.
I might have been, but you run away.
You didn't want to see it.
You brought the subject up, and I'll tell you something.
It took me three years, until 1944 when they marched us from-- I'm going ahead of myself-- from Radom to Auschwitz because they couldn't get trains anymore.
And I had hoped that maybe I'll survive.
And one of them tried to run away and they caught him.
And of course, the many German volunteers who had volunteered to shoot him right away.
And I said to my brother, I said, I better look around, I want to see what, what it is like when you shoot somebody.
I want to see it.
[SOBBING] The war was bad, indiscriminate.
And in the first few months, it wasn't-- for certain areas if you were able to hide out when they come to look for you, you managed to travel and get around.
But then they started the ghettos.
And we didn't know what they were doing in the other towns.
Unbeknown to us, they started to liquidate small towns into big cities.
But the Poles will not cooperate with you.
They wouldn't tell you because they are big on anti-Semitism themselves what was going on.
We were hidden for a day or two when I knew-- when the rumor was.
You know, in those times, there lot of rumors.
Tonight is going to be a big gathering and so on.
So We were hidden out by Poles for a day or two in certain areas.
A woman on the street.
There's no-- I don't know where it originated.
Somebody came and said-- they say tonight they're going to gather up a few hundred people.
Hide.
If it happened or not, I couldn't tell you.
With a Polish family.
Yeah, yeah, in Radom.
Some of my parents did business, some of them I knew, you know.
But we did hide.

I don't think they knew themselves what was happening, until, for many reasons, they started-- and this started in '41-- they started hanging people for no reason at all, to scare off the rest of the population.
In other words, the ammunition factory, which was now a town, which has been there for years-- I don't know how many thousands of people worked there, 4,000 or 5,000.
They would drive us by to show us they hung 10 prominent people once a week or once a month.
Just to scare the rest of them off that they don't sabotage the plan don't do something.
And everybody got scared.
That's all you need is put a scare into people.
No, some Poles too.
Some Poles too.
Yes, oh, yeah.
Before the war, the factory it was strictly-- well, I don't know if there was any Jews in there.
Maybe there were a few, you know.
But a strictly Polish factory, you know.
Not by personally, I knew them by name.
But it wasn't a one time occurrence.
This happened quite often.
Once a month.
10 People.
They set it up for 10 people.
That was it.
Whenever something went wrong, whenever something that they thought somebody sabotaged something or so on, they would pick out 10 people from the community and hang them.
Jews, yeah, and Gentiles, yeah.
It depends where the place was.
If it happened at the ammunition factory, it was mostly Gentiles.
If not, it was mostly Jews.
